section: Right to Accessibility and Service Quality
text: The quality of services provided to passengers with reduced mobility has not improved as much as the quality of services provided to passengers without disabilities. The right to accessibility is a fundamental right that should assist all citizens.
Legislation requires the adaptation of airport infrastructures and aircraft to ensure the safe and comfortable transportation of passengers with reduced mobility.

Due to global population growth and the aging process, the percentage of people with reduced mobility is expected to increase significantly, justifying increased investment in air transport accessibility.
Existing improvements in accessibility services and airport facilities must be expanded across all airports.
The primary physical obstacles for passengers with reduced mobility are located within airport terminals and at the land-air interface, specifically at aircraft boarding and deplaning points.
Dependency on third-party assistance to utilize airport services compromises the right to autonomy for passengers with reduced mobility.
The right to accessibility and non-social exclusion is a fundamental right that must be granted to all citizens.
Legislation requires the adaptation of airport infrastructure and aircraft to ensure the comfortable and safe transport of passengers with reduced mobility.
Airport infrastructure must be designed to respect accessibility concepts, enabling passengers with limited mobility to move with safety and autonomy.

Terminals, aircraft boarding and disembarking points, and airport services are identified as major obstacles in the land-air interface, often creating a dependency on third parties and violating the passenger's right to autonomy.
Identification of the various types of reduced mobility reported by respondents.
Analysis of perceptions held by persons with reduced mobility (PRM) regarding air transportation services.
Identification of physical and systemic barriers encountered by persons with reduced mobility within airport environments.
Assessment of accessibility levels and infrastructure specifically within Portuguese airports.
Procedures and perceptions regarding the emergency evacuation of persons with reduced mobility at the airport.
Identification of accessibility barriers encountered by persons with reduced mobility on board aircraft.
Evaluation of accessibility standards and services provided by airlines.
Analysis of accessibility and challenges across the different phases of a flight.
The provision of excellent services for passengers with reduced mobility is hindered by a lack of training, awareness, and sensibility among human resources.
The development of accessible infrastructures is negatively affected by a lack of policies aimed at implementing strategies to eliminate structural and communicational barriers.
Disability should be addressed by focusing on the social, economic, cultural, and political obstacles presented by society to the individual, rather than treating it solely as a medical condition.

Increasing airport accessibility can generate economic benefits and increased revenue through new market offerings while stimulating a change in mentality regarding the discrimination and marginalization of people with special needs.
The primary objective is to collect comprehensive information on airport accessibility to improve land side access and facilitate the flow of passengers with reduced mobility (PRM).
Accessibility issues in airport infrastructures must be analyzed to increase mobility for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM), contributing to social integration and the respect of citizens' rights.

The objective is to analyze the concept of reduced mobility in air transport, reflect on the state of the art regarding legislation and initiatives in Portugal and abroad, and identify measures required to comply with existing legislation.

Research involves analyzing existing interventions to find new accessibility solutions and identifying national and international guiding documents regarding accessibility for people with disabilities.
The perception of mobility-impaired persons regarding airports and services is examined through online surveys aimed at individuals with reduced mobility using air transport.
Fieldwork is conducted to collect dimensions of facilities and observe real constraints faced by Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) in airports.
The functioning of assistance services in national airports is analyzed by accompanying a person with reduced mobility during their trip.
National and international legislation establishes specific rights for people with reduced mobility (PRM).
Passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) encounter various types of barriers when attempting to travel.
Assistance services exist for passengers with reduced mobility, with operational differences observed between the services provided in Portugal and those in other countries.
Passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) are subject to specific travel restrictions and inhibitions.
According to the United Nations Universal Declaration of Human Rights, discrimination against any person based on disability is recognized as a violation of the dignity and value of the human person.
Accessibility is not associated exclusively to people with disabilities, as any person can become dependent on the help of others at any time in their life.
Accessibility is considered as a set of environmental characteristics designed to allow use by anyone, regardless of their physical, sensory, or intellectual abilities.

Accessibility means making possible the participation of persons with reduced mobility in all activities of society, encompassing all people who have a disability or physical or psychological limitation, whether temporary or permanent.
Promoting accessibility involves the elimination of barriers to provide conditions that allow the use of all equipment and spaces with autonomy and safety.

According to Article 9 of the Convention on the rights of persons with disabilities, Member States must ensure that Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) have access to the physical environment, transportation, information and communications systems, technology, and other public facilities and services on an equal basis with others.
Measures to ensure accessibility include the identification and elimination of obstacles and barriers.
In air transport, PRM journeys should follow the 'Seamless' concept, which ensures comfortable, safe, and uninterrupted travel by providing adequate assistance tailored to personal needs.
To facilitate social inclusion, passengers with reduced mobility should not be separated from passengers without reduced mobility, as accessibility in common public spaces must be guaranteed for all.
Airport environments must enable passengers to move safely and autonomously, including the provision of specific equipment if required to achieve this mobility.

Disability is recognized as an evolving concept resulting from the interaction between persons with impairments and attitudinal or environmental barriers that prevent PRM from participating in society.
Persons with disabilities include those who have physical, mental, intellectual or sensory impairment.

Discrimination based on disability refers to any distinction, exclusion or restriction based on disability that prevents or nullifies the recognition, enjoyment, or exercise of human rights and fundamental freedoms, including the denial of reasonable accommodation.
Disability can be congenital (present from birth), caused by physical or biological changes over time, or resulting from disease or accidents.

There is a distinction between reduced mobility and disability. Reduced mobility signifies a temporary or permanent limitation in mobility or an effective reduction of flexibility, coordination and perception, and does not necessarily mean that the person is disabled.

While a person with a disability is considered a Person with Reduced Mobility (PRM), a PRM is not necessarily a person with a disability, as the need for adaptation can be caused by factors other than a disability.

Elderly individuals may be classified as PRM due to aging and associated conditions—such as sclerosis, heart disease, and arthritis—which weaken joints and hinder locomotion, creating difficulties with stairs and uneven surfaces.
Obese persons may be classified as PRM due to mobility challenges when navigating environments, particularly those with steep steps or ramps.

According to Regulation (EC) no. 1107/2006, a person with reduced mobility (PRM) is any person whose mobility when using transport is reduced due to physical disability (sensory or locomotor, permanent or temporary), intellectual disability or impairment, age, or any other cause of disability, requiring appropriate attention and adaptation of services.
Persons with reduced mobility are defined by a limited ability to interact with and use their environment. Examples include the elderly, disabled persons, pregnant women, and obese persons.

Physical disability, often categorized as mobility-impaired, includes conditions such as paraplegia (total or partial paralysis of the legs and lower body), tetraplegia (total or partial paralysis of all four limbs), cerebral palsy (impaired muscle coordination caused by central nervous system damage), amputation (total or partial loss of body members), and dwarfism (small stature resulting in mobility challenges in environments designed for average or tall stature).

Visual impairment encompasses three primary categories: defective sight where objects cannot be properly seen; low vision, which is permanently reduced vision that cannot be corrected by medical methods (glasses, contact lenses, surgery) but allows for task execution; and sightlessness (total blindness).

Hearing impairment is defined as the reduction or absence of the capacity to hear sounds at various levels of intensity. The characteristics of hearing loss depend on the type and severity of the cause, as well as whether the impairment was acquired before or after the development of speech.

Intellectual or cognitive disability consists of mental functioning significantly below average originating during the development period. This is accompanied by limitations in two or more areas of adaptive behavior or everyday social and practical skills, specifically in communication, special care, family performance and commodity, independence in mobility, health and safety, school performance, leisure, and work.

Multiple disability is defined as an association of two or more disabilities, including intellectual disability associated with physical disability; hearing disability associated with intellectual and physical disability; or visual disability associated with physical disability.
Approximately 1 billion people worldwide have some form of disability, making people with reduced mobility the largest minority in the world.

The number of people with disabilities is increasing due to population growth, medical advances, and the aging process. By 2050, the global population aged 60 years and older is expected to reach 2 billion.

In Portugal, approximately 40.5% of individuals aged 15 to 64 have at least one health problem or prolonged disease, and 17.4% have difficulties performing basic activities. Among the elderly, about 50% are unable to perform all day-to-day activities, with musculoskeletal problems and mobility difficulties, specifically walking and climbing steps, being the primary issues.

Providing quality services adapted for accessibility for people with disabilities (including the elderly, sick, and children) is viewed as a strategic opportunity and a success differentiator that provides competitive advantages.

There is a significant increase in people with reduced mobility who possess the economic means to travel, driven by an aging population and policies promoting equal access to employment and integration.
It is necessary to continually and minutely study the needs of persons with special needs to ensure positive responses to their specific requirements.
Compliance with legislation and the provision of adequate airport infrastructure are essential to ensure that passengers with reduced mobility can move with autonomy and independence.

The 2006 United Nations International Convention about the Rights of Persons with Disabilities expressly prohibits all forms of discrimination based on disability, classifying such discrimination as a violation of human rights.
The implementation of strategies to minimize physical and attitudinal barriers is a responsibility of the entire community to ensure the right of all individuals to participate in society.
Airlines operating internationally must comply with local laws and regulations of the countries they serve, based on bilateral agreements.
The Constitution of 1976 enshrines the right to non-discrimination for all citizens, with Article 13 generally interpreted to include disability (Constitutional Law 1/2001).

The Prevention, Rehabilitation and Integration of People with Disabilities Law (Decreto-Lei 38/2004) and the Antidiscrimination Law (Decreto-Lei 46/2006) forbid direct and indirect discrimination based on disability and support the principle of affirmative action.
Decree-Law no. 38/2004 requires state, public, and private entities to provide information in accessible formats, such as sign language, for people with disabilities.
Under the Antidiscrimination Law (Decreto-Lei 46/2006), it is expressly forbidden to deny or limit access to public transportation.

Decree-Law no. 163/2006 requires that all transport infrastructure, including airports, be accessible to people with disabilities through the removal of barriers in roads, public spaces, collective facilities, and buildings.

Deadlines for the implementation of accessibility standards vary according to the year of facilities construction. Non-conformance with these standards after the specified deadlines is subject to sanctions.

Under Decreto-Lei 163/2006, exceptions to the elimination of architectural barriers are permitted if the action is disproportionally difficult, requires unavailable economic and financial means, or significantly affects cultural and historical patrimony.

According to Decree Law no.135/99, priority attendance must be provided to persons with reduced mobility, including the elderly, unhealthy individuals, pregnant women, people with disabilities, those accompanied by children, and other cases with priority service needs.
Pursuant to Decree Law no.145/2007, ANAC is responsible for informing users and operators in the public sector about relevant aspects concerning them.
Under Decreto-Lei 145/2007, ANAC is mandated to determine the creation of mechanisms for the appreciation of all user complaints from operators and to verify their functionality.
ANAC ensures compliance with legislation protecting the rights of air passengers, specifically Regulation (EC) No. 1107/2006 concerning the rights of passengers with reduced mobility.

Air operations are regulated by various territorial authorities: ANAC in Portugal, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) in the United States, and the European Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) in Europe.

The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) establishes general guidelines for global aviation, while the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) and the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) set strict standards and criteria for obtaining certificates, licenses, and approvals in civil aviation.

section: Emergency Preparedness for Passengers with Reduced Mobility
text: The FAA recommends that airport infrastructures, airlines, and administrators ensure that employees are fully prepared and trained to handle emergency situations and the evacuation of passengers with reduced mobility.

Bathrooms located both on-board aircraft and within airport infrastructures should be fully accessible to passengers with reduced mobility, specifically equipped with grab bars and extensible wide doors.
Airports should provide wheelchairs or other motorized equipment at the entry and exit areas of the terminal for the use of passengers with reduced mobility.
The FAA recommends the installation of ramps large enough to accommodate a wheelchair or cart at all airport stairs or gaps.
Under the Air Carrier Access Act (ACAA), foreign air carriers are obliged to provide accessible facilities, aircraft, and services for passengers with any kind of disability.

The ACAA applies to all flights traveling to and from the United States; it prohibits discrimination based on disability in air travel and mandates that air carriers provide necessary accommodations for these customers.

Airlines are prohibited from limiting the number of disabled passengers on a flight. However, they may require advance notice of up to 48 hours if specific accommodations are needed that require time for organization.
The United States Department of Transportation (DOT) interprets and applies the Access Act via regulation 14 CFR Part 382, which has been in force since May 13, 2009.
Under Regulation 14 CFR Part 382 and EU Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006, airlines are prohibited from refusing a passenger based on their disability, except for duly justified safety reasons.
Regulation 14 CFR Part 382 permits airlines to legally require a passenger to travel with a safety assistant when a request for physical help has been made.
Appropriate equipment and means must be used to provide boarding and deplaning assistance based on safety and the specific needs of the passenger.
Airlines may require a medical certificate confirming that a passenger is clinically stable if there are doubts regarding the passenger's autonomy to travel safely.
Terminal facilities providing access to air transport must be designed to be easily accessible and usable by persons with reduced mobility.
All audio-visual monitors located within the terminal must include high-contrast subtitles to ensure information is accessible to all passengers.
The UK Department for Transport requires that employees receive adequate training for their specific functions and possess knowledge of relevant legal rights.
Training for employees must begin before they start their job and be updated at regular intervals.

Training must include information on hidden or barely visible deficiencies and the necessary skills for providing assistance to passengers with reduced mobility, including handling unexpected situations.

Employee training should address attitudinal, environmental, and organizational barriers faced by people with disabilities, suggest proposals to eliminate these difficulties, and establish access audits.
Regulation (EC) no.1107/2006 reinforces the rights of passengers with disabilities and reduced mobility on air transport within the European Union.
Under EU regulations, all airports in the European Union should be comparable in terms of accessibility for passengers with disabilities or reduced mobility.

Regulation (EC) no.1107/2006 does not affect other passenger rights established by other regulations, specifically Regulation (EC) no. 261/2004 regarding compensation and assistance for denied boarding, flight cancellation, or delays.

section: Exercise of Repayment and Rerouting Rights
text: If an event grants similar rights to repayment or rerouting under both Regulation 1107/2006 and Regulation 261/2004, the passenger must choose and exercise that right only once.
Passengers should notify the airline of their particular needs at least 48 hours in advance of the flight.
In the absence of prior notification, the managing body shall make all reasonable efforts to provide the necessary assistance.

Assistance must enable disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRM) to proceed from a designated point of arrival in the airport to an aircraft, and from the aircraft to a designated point of departure, including boarding and deplaning.
At clearly identified points, PRMs must be able to obtain basic airport information, announce their arrival, and request assistance.

section: Right to Equal Travel and Free Assistance
text: Persons with reduced mobility shall have travel opportunities similar to other citizens. Adequate assistance and necessary equipment must be provided at the airport and inside the aircraft to support specific needs, free of charge.
Airport management entities are responsible for passenger assistance, which must be organized to avoid interruptions and delays.
Air transport information must be provided in the same languages available to other passengers, using alternative formats accessible to all persons with disabilities and reduced mobility.
Passengers must be compensated for the loss or damage of wheelchairs or other mobility equipment occurring during handling at the airport or on-board the aircraft.
Airlines and airport authorities must ensure that all staff providing direct assistance to disabled persons and PRMs possess the knowledge required to meet their specific needs.
All airport staff in direct contact with passengers must receive training on equality of treatment and disability awareness. This training must be provided upon recruitment and updated periodically.

Regulation (EC) no. 1107/2006 mandates that airport infrastructure and aircraft be designed to be accessible to Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) and requires the renovation of existing infrastructure to accommodate PRM needs.
The regulation prohibits denying reservations or boarding to elderly or disabled passengers based on discrimination and ensures their entitlement to free assistance in airports and on board aircraft.
Transport may be denied to passengers for justified reasons, including security reasons, legal requirements, or physical impossibility due to the size of the aircraft or its doors.
If an airline, agent, or tour operator denies boarding or refuses a booking for justified reasons, they must make efforts to propose an acceptable alternative to the passenger.
Passengers with reduced mobility and their accompanying support persons are entitled to reimbursement if boarding was denied based on the passenger's disability condition.
Passengers who believe the regulation has been infringed may submit claims to the airport operator, the air carrier, or the Member State organism responsible for applying sanctions.
Member States are responsible for supervising and ensuring full compliance with the provisions of Regulation (EC) no. 1107/2006.
Member States are required to establish and guarantee the application of legal sanctions for infringements of the Regulation.
Services for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PMR) must be professional and seamless, ensuring no points exist where passengers are abandoned or unattended.

Airport and aircraft operators should consult and involve organizations representing people with special needs when developing and assessing services, training programs, and the design of systems and equipment to ensure equal access to air transport.
The body responsible for enforcement must conduct regular customer service checks to ensure standards are maintained, improved, and that the rights of persons with reduced mobility are respected.
All staff providing air transport services must receive training to ensure they understand their responsibilities and can provide quality and respectful service to people with disabilities.

Training must cover behavioral, environmental, physical, and organizational barriers affecting PMR in air transport, as well as emergency evacuation procedures for passengers with special needs. It should include an understanding of diversity across various disabilities, including learning, cognitive, physical, hearing, and visual impairments.
Aircraft and airport operators are recommended to provide training regularly, specifically every one to two years, to refresh staff knowledge.
Staff must be aware of existing national and international legislation and regulations regarding passengers with reduced mobility, in accordance with ICAO (2013).

Guidance material on air transport must be published in formats and technologies suitable for different types of disabilities, including electronic, web-based, large print, and audio, provided without additional charge.

Published accessibility information must include: hours of operation, designated parking, local drop-off and pick-up areas, accessibility phone numbers, wheelchair or electric cart services, animal relief areas, inter-terminal transport, accessible ground transportation, companion travel policies, complaint resolution services, notice requirements, check-in and departure times, requirements for locomotion aids, and available airport and in-flight services, including loading equipment.
Information counters must be accessible to all persons with reduced mobility (PRM).
Airport and aircraft operators must ensure that check-in kiosks under their control are accessible and identified with the universal symbol of accessibility.
Public announcements regarding delays, schedule changes, gate changes, connections, and baggage claims must be delivered both verbally and visually.
Information monitors must be installed to ensure visibility and avoid glare for persons with reduced mobility, including those using wheelchairs.
Information displayed on monitors must use simple, accessible language and avoid the use of acronyms.
Airport operators must adhere to accessibility standards throughout all phases of planning, construction, and renovation of airport facilities.
Seating must be provided in waiting areas for people with special needs, ensuring that seats are not placed in locations that block evacuation routes.
Entities must ensure that no new barriers are created and that any existing obstructions are removed.

Airport authorities must incorporate wayfinding methods appropriate for people with disabilities, including visual contrast, tactile patterns on floors and walls indicating direction, and the strategic arrangement of architectural elements like walls and columns.
Airports must ensure there is a designated area available for service animals to relieve themselves.
Airport operators must provide clear signage to enable people with special needs to navigate the airport and find toilets, emergency exits, elevators, stairs, and accessible communication systems.
Signs must use letters or symbols that are free of glare, utilize high-contrast colors, and employ a universal font and size.
Airport signage must be supplemented by Braille wherever possible.
Signs must be placed where they are easily visible to Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM), including wheelchair users.
Aircraft and airport management teams must provide regular updates to PRM on flight status and inquire about their needs, for example, every 30 minutes.
All safety procedures must be reported either verbally or visually, depending on the specific needs of the individual.

Depending on national security regulations, airport operators must provide temporary passes to allow PRM to be accompanied by a person of their choice through secured areas and boarding gates, in addition to aircraft operator or terminal personnel.

People needing assistance should have the opportunity to board before other passengers and deplane after them to ensure a more dignified, less stressful experience and improved operational efficiency.

Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) have the right to assistance during boarding and deplaning. This includes transfers from personal wheelchairs to boarding chairs or other mobility aids, movement to and from airplane seats, assistance with baggage, support during immigration and customs procedures, and help in public areas such as bathrooms and stairs.
Airport staff must provide assistance to PRMs upon request, specifically to help them reach connection flights when schedules are short.

Aircraft construction and refurbishment must maximize accessibility for PRMs. Required design features include accessible bathrooms, tactile directional signage for visually impaired passengers, and boarding stairs equipped with handrails on both sides and anti-slip surfaces.
Aircraft must have seats designated as accessible for PRMs. These seats may only be occupied by other passengers at the last moment.

Aircraft must provide seating with sufficient floor space for service animals at the passenger's feet. If no appropriate seat is available, operators must suggest alternatives, such as another flight with more spacious seating. No costs shall be charged for the transport of service animals.
Safety briefings or other information must be provided individually for persons with reduced mobility, as requested or required in order to meet passenger needs.

According to Regulation (EC) no. 1107/2006, a disabled person may be required to travel with an assistant for security reasons, such as the ability to understand and respond to safety briefings or to assist in an emergency evacuation.
Passengers who require personal care, such as assistance with feeding or bathroom use, must travel with an assistant because airline staff do not provide personal care services.

If mobility aids are damaged or lost and unavailable upon arrival, airport operators must provide a temporary and free replacement until the equipment is repaired or the person is refunded according to applicable conventions.

Airport and aircraft operators must establish procedures for people with disabilities to submit verbal and written complaints. These procedures must be accessible and support assistive technology, such as TTYs.
Airports must maintain a quality policy that ensures international quality standards in infrastructure and provides services that maximize the satisfaction of passengers with reduced mobility.

section: Challenges for Passengers with Reduced Mobility
text: Passengers with mobility problems or disabilities are particularly susceptible to fatigue and disorientation resulting from traveling long distances and navigating unfamiliar airport facilities.

The standard airport process for a passenger includes arrival via public transport, taxi, or car; check-in and hold baggage registration; identification and boarding pass verification; passage through security and x-ray; and monitoring gate screens for boarding.
Assistance services for passengers with special needs are available at airports, such as the MyWay service at national airports and similar assistance services provided within EU Member States.

Evaluating airport accessibility for passengers with reduced mobility requires assessment of: transport modes for airport access, circulation areas, departure and arrival car park slots, personalized attendance, boarding passes, check-in, waiting areas, PRM loading and unloading equipment, communication and signage, elevators, toilets, and general circulation spaces.
Social inclusion requires guaranteeing access for all people without discrimination to normal societal activities.
Passengers with Reduced Mobility (PRM) face travel challenges including negative social attitudes, architectural structures, and policies that create psychological and physical barriers.
PRM face a lack of accessibility due to external barriers and obstacles found within infrastructures.

Personal acceptance of a disability is a critical factor for active societal participation; failure to accept one's condition can lead to the avoidance of public spaces and reduced flight frequencies.
Family support is a primary influence on daily decisions and travel; a lack of familial support creates additional resistance that the PRM must overcome to fly.

Personnel must maintain awareness of the needs of passengers with disabilities and the ability to communicate them effectively to remove barriers concerning accessibility information and accessible facilities.

Personnel involved in the design, management, and provision of services must have a precise understanding of how their work affects people with disabilities and possess the knowledge and ability to ensure that people with reduced mobility are included.
Airport operators and airlines must provide an environment without barriers at the airport terminal and on board the aircraft.
Airline and airport staff must be specially trained to manage emergency situations, specifically the evacuation of passengers with reduced mobility.
Airport terminals and aircraft (with at least one toilet on board) must be equipped with toilets featuring expansion ports and grab bars.
Wheelchairs or other motorized equipment must be available for use by persons with reduced mobility (PRM) at the arrival and departure areas of the airport.
Stairs and ramps must be provided at every change of level within the airport.
All ramps should be large enough to accommodate a wheelchair or trolley.
Adapted toilets and wheelchairs for PRM movement inside the cabin are typically only provided on flights with a duration of more than 5 hours.
Airline websites often lack specific accessibility information and references to emergency evacuation procedures for passengers with reduced mobility (PRM).
Airport floors and accessibility infrastructures must be anti-slip and regularly checked to prevent falls for passengers with reduced mobility.
Ramps and elevators must be consistently accessible, and wheelchairs should be available near entrance and exit ramps for PRM use.
Ramps must be kept free of obstacles to ensure accessibility for persons with reduced mobility.
To ensure an accessible experience, aircraft should provide adapted toilets and on-board wheelchairs to facilitate movement between cabin seats and restrooms.
Airlines typically reserve high-priority seats for passengers with reduced mobility (PRM), usually located near bathrooms and exit doors.
Priority and assistance during the loading and unloading of passengers with reduced mobility is essential for safety.
Passengers with reduced mobility should have priority or separate treatment in waiting areas and have access to exclusive desks to support their specific needs.
Critical accessibility factors for passengers with reduced mobility include the distance between the parking lot and the terminal, as well as accessibility between terminals.

Training programs for personnel assisting passengers with disabilities must include: attitudinal, environmental, and organizational barriers and their solutions; principles of accessibility audits; information on complex, hidden, or less visible disabilities; and the specific skills required to assist passengers with special needs.
Staff training must prepare employees to handle unexpected situations, be provided before the employee starts their functions, and be updated at regular intervals.
General deterioration in physical and mental functions associated with advancing years can negatively impact an elderly traveler's ability to navigate airport terminals.
Technological proficiency varies among the elderly; young seniors typically handle technology more easily than older seniors.
Signage is a significant factor that increases journey complexity, often making it difficult for passengers to locate facilities or services, especially within large airports.
Elderly passengers using remote parking facilities face challenges including exposure to weather, long walking distances to terminal entrances, and potential long waits for shuttle buses.
Increased fatigue in aging passengers leads to difficulties in managing baggage and navigating check-in and security areas.

Check-in and security points are high-stress areas for elderly passengers due to prolonged standing, waiting in lines, and the physical requirement of depositing clothing and handling luggage in tubs.
Long walking distances required to reach gates or move between gates are a frequent problem for aging passengers.
Some terminals are reducing the number of escalators and replacing them with more elevators and ramps to improve accessibility.

section: Definition of Passengers with Reduced Mobility
text: Elderly passengers are commonly classified as passengers with reduced mobility due to frequent hearing and vision impairments, mobility difficulties, and challenges in adapting to technological or automation advances.

Special requirements for elderly passengers must extend beyond flight cancellation and delay announcements to include information regarding emergency exits, special senior meals, terminal directions, and transportation to and from the airport.
Increased automation of check-in counters and kiosks can complicate air travel for elderly passengers, who may struggle with booking services such as catering and seat selection.

Decreased cognitive abilities and loss of vision and hearing can hamper an elderly passenger's ability to understand and follow airport instructions, often leading to disorientation or difficulty traveling long distances within the terminal.
Constricted toilet facilities serve as a significant barrier that may discourage elderly passengers from traveling.

Key service requirements for passengers include the attitude of check-in and ground service staff, user-friendly boarding processes, seat selection options, exclusive customs counters, and priority boarding.
Critical needs for elderly passengers that are frequently unmet by airport infrastructure include the provision of special meals and the transmission of relevant information.

Providing services for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) presents operational challenges, including a lack of specialized equipment, high physical demands on staff, and the requirement for additional time per customer.
There is a market requirement for new or modified assistive products, specifically flexible mobility equipment tailored for travel and leisure activities.
The decision to travel for passengers with disabilities is heavily influenced by the existence of external barriers and obstacles encountered throughout the journey.

The intention to travel is influenced by the ability to overcome intrapersonal constraints, such as lack of travel desire or religious beliefs, and interpersonal constraints, such as a lack of company.

Travel intention can be inhibited by a lack of information regarding available services, specifically wheelchair services, seat selection, and airport facility accessibility, as well as a lack of opportunities or time.

section: Barriers for Passengers with Reduced Mobility
text: Passengers with reduced mobility face several inhibiting factors, including environmental barriers (attitudinal, architectural, and economical), interactive and communication barriers, and intrinsic barriers associated with physical, psychological, or cognitive functioning.

Cultural and social attitudes toward reduced mobility vary globally and can represent significant obstacles; negative and discriminatory attitudes can lead individuals to internalize these views, affecting their self-perception.
Passengers with disabilities may have limited resistance to unpredictable situations, meaning flight delays or cancellations can cause significant discomfort compared to other passengers.
Extended delays at airports can lead to the failure of powered mobility equipment, such as batteries running down, which can leave passengers with severe disabilities immobile.
Passengers who have recently undergone surgery may experience reduced stamina, hindering their ability to maneuver through airport crowds and terminals.
Airport operators and airlines may fail to deliver requested wheelchairs in a timely manner or may fail to provide them entirely.
There are occurrences where wheelchairs and other essential mobility equipment are not returned to the passenger intact.

The quality of the first travel experience is a critical factor in determining whether a passenger with disabilities will continue to travel or abandon the desire to do so based on whether constraints and barriers were successfully overcome.
Repeated negative experiences with aviation accessibility can lead to a loss of self-confidence, lack of control, and feelings of helplessness regarding air travel.

Information regarding services and regulations for passengers with reduced mobility varies across airline websites, often requiring passengers to contact reservation centers directly for specific details.
The lack of information provided in accessible formats is a significant barrier that discourages people with disabilities from traveling by air.

Passengers with reduced mobility may encounter difficulties with online reservations. When making reservations at check-in counters, they may be required to explain their medical condition, provide a medical certificate, or may be refused boarding.
Physical barriers are frequently found in gate area facilities, including restaurants, shops, restrooms, bathrooms, and lounges.
Disabled passengers may experience delays and accessibility issues while waiting in lines for passport control and safety inspections.

section: Impact of Facilities on Travel Volume
text: Insufficient facilities and services discourage people with disabilities from using air transport; eliminating barriers in airport infrastructure and during flights would increase the percentage of passengers with reduced mobility using these services.
MyWay is a service providing personalized assistance to passengers with reduced mobility using air travel to, from, or transiting through an airport located within a member state of the EU.
The MyWay service must be requested at least 48 hours in advance of the flight to ensure that information is forwarded by the airline to the airports involved to provide the necessary assistance.

For MyWay passengers at the moment of boarding, assistance wait time targets are: 80% of passengers should wait no longer than 10 minutes, 90% no longer than 20 minutes, and 100% no longer than 30 minutes.
For MyWay passengers during deplaning, assistance wait time targets are: 80% of passengers should wait no longer than 5 minutes, 90% no longer than 10 minutes, and 100% no longer than 20 minutes.
In the absence of a previous request, passengers must not wait more than 45 minutes for assistance after the beginning of landing.

Under ISO15845:2014, a person with reduced mobility (PRM) boarding a vehicle can be accompanied by at least one attendant, and the design of the vehicle must consider safety and psychological aspects.
Services must prioritize the passenger's feelings of well-being and security, physical comfort, and the avoidance of panic (ISO 15845:2014).
Passengers with reduced mobility should arrive at the airport at least 2 hours in advance, locate the Designated Points of Arrival via signage, and inform the MyWay service of their arrival.
The MyWay service can be requested at several access points via provided phones; passengers must identify themselves to the staff to be located.
Passengers arriving from another airport must follow the same identification and request process to activate the assistance service.

Passengers with reduced mobility are entitled to personal assistance and luggage help, including accompaniment through check-in, security controls, border control points, and boarding, until they reach their seats on the plane.
The accompaniment service does not prevent passengers from using duty-free shops, provided a meeting time is agreed upon between the passenger and the MyWay staff.

For deplaning, the airline must notify the airport of the need for MyWay service. Passengers are entitled to personal and baggage assistance from their aircraft seat to a designated point of departure located in the arrivals area.

The MyWay service is provided free of charge to the user and includes both qualified professionals and mechanical means to facilitate mobility, such as airport vans, boarding bridges, ambulifts, or onboard wheelchairs.
Airport vans are utilized specifically when there is a significant walking distance between the terminal and the aircraft.
The airport boarding bridge is a device that connects the airport terminal to the aircraft, providing a tunnel for passenger entry and exit that protects them from rain and wind.
The ambulift is a vehicle equipped with an elevator that transports the passenger directly to the aircraft entrance.
Due to aircraft interior dimensions, passengers must be transferred to a special aisle chair with the assistance of trained staff to reach their assigned seat.
A passenger's personal wheelchair is stored in the aircraft hold with the baggage and returned upon arrival. During deplaning, the passenger must be transferred back to their own mobility equipment.

The ELO System, implemented in Brazil, focuses on adapting airport facilities to make infrastructure accessible to Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM), whereas the MyWay service focuses on direct passenger assistance.

The ELO system consists of acclimatized connectors designed for ground-level connection between the gate and the aircraft, enabling all passengers, including those with reduced mobility, to board and deplane with comfort, safety, accessibility, and autonomy.
The final part of the ELO connector system includes stairs and a lift adapted for passengers with special needs, with a weight capacity of up to 225 kg.
Within the European Union, the rights of passengers with reduced mobility are ensured by a common regulation applicable to all Member States.

Passengers whose rights are not respected should first contact the corresponding airline or airport. If necessary, a complaint can be submitted to the national authority of the Member State where the problem occurred.

The Portuguese Civil Aviation Authority is the body responsible for enforcing passenger rights for flights departing from national airports and flights from other countries to national airports operated by Community air carriers.
Companies operating outside the EU, regardless of their nationality, must respect existing EU accessibility rules when providing services within the European space.
Despite specific legislation for people with reduced mobility, airlines may refuse boarding or impose restrictions based on security reasons.
Law requires that priority boarding be given to passengers with reduced mobility, including in situations of flight delay, cancellation, or overbooking.
The European Commission recommends that passengers inform the airline of their condition at least 48 hours in advance of the flight to allow for the management of the service.
Passenger assistance services are free for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM), as the costs of care are distributed equally among all passengers.
Companies may only demand a medical certificate if there is a risk to the health or safety of the passenger, other passengers, or the crew.
Requiring a PRM to travel with a companion is only legal when the passenger is unable to fulfill security and autonomy requirements.
Airlines are not obligated to provide assistance with meals, the administration of medication during flight, or personal care.

Main barriers dissuading PRM from traveling include the attitude of service staff, lack of specialized personnel, architectural barriers, lack of information in accessible formats, and lack of safety information regarding emergency evacuation.
Continuous training in accessibility is essential for all professionals and staff working with people with special needs to ensure better assistance and respect for their rights.

To ensure social inclusion and that persons with disabilities exercise their rights and fundamental freedoms on an equal basis with others, reasonable adjustments—defined as appropriate and rational modifications—must be implemented.
To ensure the best possible assistance, Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) should plan their journey in advance and notify the airline of all their specific needs prior to travel.
To address the lack of accessibility in air travel, barriers must be identified across four distinct phases: pre-travel, before the flight, during the flight, and after the flight.

The analysis of PRM accessibility conditions and assistance services consists of two primary methods: conducting online surveys to understand PRM perception and performing practical trips between airports to evaluate the actual delivery of assistance services.
Lisbon and Oporto airport infrastructures are analyzed using the 'Mobility and Accessibility for All' guide, which is based on Decreto Lei no.163/2006 (SNRIPD, 2009).

The primary objectives are to facilitate the inclusion of mobility-impaired persons in air transport by examining their needs, assessing accessibility conditions at national airports, and evaluating the assistance services provided to these passengers.

The accessibility survey is divided into three sections: demographic data (nationality, age, gender, type of reduced mobility), personal opinions on airport infrastructure, and accessibility within the aeroplane.

The survey employs multiple-choice and open-answer questions. Constraints in airports and aeroplanes are measured using a rating scale: Very important, Important, Indifferent, Something Important, Nothing important, and Do Not Know.
To reach a representative sample of respondents with reduced mobility, the online survey was distributed via associations and institutions specialized in reduced mobility.

The survey was disseminated via email to national institutions and conducted in collaboration with organizations including the Salvador Association, APCC, centre for independent living, Lisbon Inaccessible, APD, ADFA, Portal of Disabled Citizens, Centre for Rehabilitation and Integration of Disabled People, Senior Academy (Covilh), INR, and Foundation LIGA.

Between 28 July 2015 and 28 July 2016, 164 valid questionnaires were collected using Google Forms. Data were stored in a single database table and analyzed using SPSS 23 for Microsoft Windows and Microsoft Excel 2010.
Participants were characterized based on four variables: nationality, age, gender, and type of reduced mobility.
Nationality, age, and gender variables were analyzed using cross-reference tables, crossing each variable with the question: 'Do you have any type of reduced mobility?'.
Out of 164 participants (160 valid responses), 129 were Portuguese (58 with reduced mobility), 13 were Brazilian (5 with reduced mobility), and 4 were Spanish (1 with reduced mobility).
Out of 164 survey participants, 68 individuals reported having reduced mobility. This group includes participants of Angolan, Argentine, and French nationality.

The highest incidence of reduced mobility occurs in the 26-32 age group (10 individuals), followed by the 32-38 age group (8 individuals). The lowest frequency of reduced mobility is observed in the 68-74 age group.

Among the 65 respondents with reduced mobility, 52 are under the age of 65 and 13 are 65 years or older. Among the 95 participants without reduced mobility, 74 are under the age of 65 and 21 are 65 years or older.
Of 164 valid responses, 89 participants are men, 39 of whom reported reduced mobility. Of the 75 female participants, 29 reported reduced mobility.
Out of 164 survey respondents, 41% (68 persons) reported having some type of reduced mobility, while 59% (96 persons) reported having no reduced mobility.
Among respondents without reduced mobility, 19% (31 persons) have accompanied a person with reduced mobility (PRM) during an air trip, while 40% (65 persons) have never accompanied a PRM.

Among the 68 participants with reduced mobility, the distribution of disabilities is as follows: physical disability (75.0%, 48 persons), sensory impairment (15.6% total, consisting of 10.9% visual and 4.7% hearing disabilities), intellectual disability/mental diseases (3.1%, 2 persons), and multiple disabilities (6.3%, 4 persons).
Types of reduced mobility include physical disability, hearing disability, visual disability, intellectual disability, and multiple disabilities.

section: PRM Perception of Air Transport Accessibility
text: 51.5% of Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) believe that air transport is not an easy mode of transportation, while 27.9% consider it accessible.
64.7% of PRM respondents report that information regarding services for PRM on airport and airline websites is hard to obtain, whereas 22.1% find it easy.
Approximately 39.7% of respondents indicate that PRM prefer other modes of transportation over flying.
86.8% of respondents state there is a need for regulatory changes to obligate airports and airlines to guarantee a specific level of autonomy for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM).

section: Impact of Service Quality on Travel Frequency
text: Approximately 66.2% of Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) indicated that they would travel more frequently if the quality of service provided by airports and airlines were improved.
Bad previous experiences have led 29.4% of PRM to give up flying with specific airlines or through certain airports.
Some PRM do not switch to alternative modes of transport because other transportation options are also perceived as inaccessible.
PRM encounter discrimination from both employees and other passengers, driven by a lack of awareness and respect toward individuals with reduced mobility.
ATM and check-in counters at Lisbon Airport are reported as inadequate for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM).
Lisbon Airport is reported as being too large to be traveled on foot.
The airport shuttle between Terminal 1 and Terminal 2 at Lisbon Airport, as well as the subway, are reported as completely inaccessible to PRM.
Terminal 2 suffers from a lack of parking, insufficient areas for eating and resting, and poor toilet cleanliness.
There is a reported lack of adequate signage and tactile flooring for navigation.
Existing legislation and measures must be reinforced to improve accessibility both within airports and inside aircraft.
Air transport should provide a service without accessibility barriers or discrimination, designed as if all passengers were Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM).
Requirements for PRM support include specialized staff for wheelchair transfers, assistance for needs before boarding, and staff to assist PRM with shopping or toilet access.
Congestion and a lack of civic spirit in the Shuttle T2 result in passengers pushing others while entering and exiting the vehicle.
Qualified staff and interpreters in sign language should be available in the terminal to improve integration and safety for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM).
The airport should utilize systems such as MyWay and ELO to enable PRM to navigate to the aircraft autonomously, ensuring access points are easy to find.
Eating areas, ATM machines, and elevators must be fully prepared and accessible for people with reduced mobility.
Essential improvements to airport signage and the implementation of tactile flooring are required to allow PRM to orient themselves independently.
PRM should be permitted to use their own mobility equipment until the time of boarding, and airport-provided wheelchairs must be maintained in optimal condition.
Priority boarding protocols must be strictly followed to ensure PRM have the necessary space and conditions to accommodate themselves during boarding and departure.
The boarding and deplaning process must be conducted in an accessible environment using appropriate equipment, eliminating the use of stairs.
To minimize delays, airlines should utilize boarding bridges or provide their own assistance equipment to facilitate PRM boarding.
To manage long walking distances to the aircraft, the airport should provide mechanical walkways, elevators, special chairs, or free transport vehicles.
Clear and comprehensive information must be provided regarding available services for PRM, including specific procedures for emergency evacuations.
Staff must be trained in the proper handling of mobility equipment and luggage for passengers with special needs to prevent damage and ensure effective problem-solving.
There is a lack of transport means designed or specially adapted for persons with reduced mobility, such as taxis, to facilitate access to the airport.

Procedures requiring 48 hours advance notice for check-in and hold baggage delivery should be simplified for persons with limited mobility to reduce undesired waiting times and align with normal check-in procedures.
Airlines should not limit the number of persons with reduced mobility (PRM) and accompanying persons allowed per flight, or should clearly disclose the specific PRM capacity for each flight.
On-board assistance staff must be trained in the procedures required for the transport of persons with reduced mobility, and airlines should provide qualified staff proficient in sign language.
Aisle chairs are currently restricted to availability on long-haul flights.
Improved signage and tactile flooring are essential within the aeroplane and airport to allow persons with reduced mobility to orient themselves independently.
Seats must be designed for easier access to facilitate transfers and toilet visits for persons with reduced mobility.
There is a reported lack of reserved seats for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM), specifically regarding the availability of seats located near the aircraft entrance or exit.
Reduced space between seats causes discomfort for PRM, particularly for blind passengers who must accommodate an assistance dog.
It is suggested that PRM should be allowed to travel in business class at the same price as economy to facilitate easier transfers, movements, and closer access to toilets.
Constraints include difficulties in using emergency exits and insufficient space within aircraft toilets, which also compromises passenger privacy.
Key barriers for PRM within airports include a lack of specialized and trained personnel and inadequate airport information and assistance services.
Critical constraints for PRM during the airport experience include challenges with transfers at the airport terminal, baggage claim, check-in, and boarding areas.
Areas identified as less significant constraints for PRM include Duty Free shops, waiting areas, and lavatories.

Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) face barriers across various airport touchpoints, including Baggage Claim, Boarding, Check-in, Duty Free, Emergency exits, Security, Transfers, Waiting Areas, Information Areas, Shop Exits, and a lack of specialized assistance and trained personnel or lavatories.
Of the 58 PRM respondents who used Lisbon Airport, 32 (55.2%) consider that the infrastructure does not provide sufficient conditions to ensure autonomy and comfort.
Of the 22 PRM respondents who used Oporto Airport, 12 (54.5%) do not consider the airport to be accessible.
In an analysis of five airports, Ponta Delgada airport was rated as accessible by all users, whereas Funchal airport was not considered accessible by any of the respondents.
Approximately 68% of Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) reported that they would be unable to evacuate an airport autonomously in an emergency situation.

There is a reported deficiency in emergency communication for PRM; 80.4% of respondents stated there is insufficient information regarding evacuation procedures, and 89.1% indicated they would feel safer if airports provided accessible information about emergency plans and exits.

Significant obstacles for PRM within aircraft include boarding and deplaning transfers (68.2%), limited space between seats (67.8%), lavatory accessibility (63.7%), general mobility inside the aircraft (63.7%), and seat transfers (59.8%).

The most critical constraints for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) within the aircraft are seat transfers (88.5%), the space between seats (87.8%), boarding/deplaning transfers (85.2%), lavatory accessibility (84.6%), and general mobility inside the aircraft (84.6%).
Issues identified as less critical for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) include seat-belt constraints (22.8%) and the accommodation of carry-on baggage (13%).

Survey data regarding Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) indicates varying levels of perceived accessibility among airlines: 44.4% of TAP passengers, 89.5% of Ryanair passengers, and 30% of EasyJet passengers consider their respective airlines to have insufficient conditions to offer autonomy and comfort.

section: Definition of Accessibility for Special Needs
text: For persons with special needs, accessibility is defined as the ability to utilize airport areas and aircraft with security and autonomy.
The improvement of accessibility for passengers with special needs is centered on facilitating access to airport services.

Decree-Law 163/2006 of 8 August establishes normative provisions to improve accessibility for persons with reduced mobility by removing barriers in public spaces, collective facilities, buildings, and airports.

The Airport Service Quality (ASQ) program, developed with Airports Council International, aims to improve airport services via quality management certification (ASQ Assured) and passenger satisfaction evaluations (ASQ Survey).

Compliance with accessibility legislation is verified using the 'Mobility and Accessibility for All' guide, which provides the specific dimensions for facilities required to interpret and implement Decree-Law 163/2006.
Passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) can access Lisbon airport via taxi, Aerobus, Carris buses, or the metropolitan system.

The Aerobus is classified as accessible. The Lisbon metropolitan system includes accessible stations, including the airport station. Carris operates accessible buses serving the airport, specifically lines 705, 722, 744, and 783.
Adapted taxi vehicles for PRM are provided by operators including Auto Taxis Serra D'Arga Lda and Auto Coope Cooperativa de Taxis de Lisboa.
Temporary parking spaces in the Terminal 1 arrivals area meet the minimum dimension requirement of 5x2.5m, although they are not marked with contrasting colors on the floor surface.

Temporary parking spaces are marked with a horizontal accessibility signal (without contrasting color), a vertical sign featuring the symbol of accessibility, and a vertical sign indicating the MyWay service meeting point.
In the reserved parking area (P2) of Terminal 1, reserved spaces exceed the minimum required dimensions of 5x2.5m.
The number of places reserved for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM), compared with the total number of places for people without reduced mobility, complies with legal requirements.
Reserved accessibility spaces are marked with contrasting colors on the floor, featuring one horizontal accessibility sign and one vertical sign with the accessibility symbol.
There are no parking spaces available in Terminal 2.
In both Terminal 1 and Terminal 2, the height of kerbs near pedestrian crossings is no higher than 0.12m, with a kerb downgrade of 0.02m.
The minimum width of pavements in areas with obstacles is at least 1.5m.
Airport floors provide good adherence, and the height of fixed signposts is 2m.

The interception of islands in the middle of zebra crossings with pedestrian crossings must have a length of 1.5m or more, and the island width must be no less than the width of the pedestrian crossing.
Pedestrian crossing surfaces have a different texture than pavements and roads, but they lack the contrasting color recommended by ACAPO.
There are no traffic lights at the pedestrian crossings.
Internal airport circulation areas include stairs, ramps, travellators, escalators, and elevators between floors.
The airport lacks directional and alert tactile floor signage, which is a recommendation of the ICAO manual.
In Terminal 1 and Terminal 2, there is a lack of Braille materials at information and check-in counters, in commercial areas, and on aircraft safety equipment.
Signs must feature letters in a contrasting colour, be free of brightness, and be positioned at a height of 2m from floor level to indicate the location of elevators and accessible bathrooms.

The departures area ramp in Terminal 1 has a width of 1.2m, a horizontal projection of approximately 12m, and overcomes a level difference of 1.20m. It is equipped with handrails at a height of 0.95m and lateral protections of 0.1m.
The Terminal 1 departure ramp is non-compliant because it has a slope of approximately 10% and lacks a rest platform in the middle of the ramp.
The interior curved ramp is compliant with accessibility guides, featuring an 8% incline, a radius of curvature of no less than 3m, and a floor with good adhesion.
Ramps along pedestrian crossings have a width greater than 1.2m, an inclination of 8%, a gap of 0.12m, and a horizontal projection of 1.5m.
Ramps in Terminal 2 used to overcome kerb differences of 0.12m are compliant with the law, featuring an inclination of 8%, a horizontal projection of 1.5m, and a width of 0.90m.
In Terminal 1, all stairs must have a width of no less than 1.50m.
Stairs in Terminal 1 should be equipped with handrails on both sides with a height of 0.90m and a diameter of 0.05m; currently, two stairs are non-compliant as they lack handrails.
While all stairs in Terminal 1 have sideguards, they lack the recommended approach areas (distinguished by texture or colour) at the start of the stairs.

Only two existing stairs comply with the recommendation of having a single, continuous strip with a width of 0.04m and different texture and color contrast on the edge of the steps. Other stairs feature multiple rough strips without color contrast, which is not in accordance with recommendations.
Stairs have a maximum height of 0.16m between steps and a minimum depth of 0.28m, with dimensions remaining constant across each staircase.
All stairs include rest platforms with a depth greater than 1m and a width at least equal to the width of the steps.

Only two of the six stairs have handrails that extend 0.3m beyond the last step parallel to the floor and extend beyond the first step by a length equal to the step depth while maintaining the slope of the stairs.
There are no stairs located in Terminal 2.
Lifts in Terminal 1 meet or exceed the minimum recommended size of 1.10m x 1.40m, and all doorways have a minimum usable width of 0.90m.
An obstacle-free area of at least 1.5m x 1.5m is provided in front of the lift entrance doors.
Call buttons are positioned at a height of 1.2m, although they are not consistently located on the right side of the door.
Internal control buttons are positioned at a height between 0.9m and 1.3m.
Lift control buttons include Braille, raised characters, and surrounding luminous devices.
Cabin grab bars are installed at a height of 0.9m and are positioned 0.05m from the wall.
Lifts are not equipped with sound information systems.
There are no passenger lifts in Terminal 2 as it consists of only one floor; existing lifts are for staff use only.
All doors must have a width of 0.90m or more and must not use revolving handles.
Locks and pullers must be located at a height of 1.10m.
Automatic outside doors must have a width of no less than 2m.
The width of building aisles must be greater than 1.8m.
Aisles in the waiting rooms of Terminal 1 and Terminal 2 must correspond to the minimum widths of 0.9m and 1.5m as specified in Annex 2.
There are currently no spaces reserved for passengers in wheelchairs in the waiting rooms.
Toilets in Terminal 1 and Terminal 2 must respect minimum area requirements, generally with dimensions greater than or equal to 2.2m x 2.2m.
Toilets must provide access from both sides of the bowl with vertical folding double supports at heights of 0.65m and 0.8m, extending approximately 0.20m beyond the front edge of the bowl.
The toilet bowl must be located at a height of 0.45m relative to the floor, with the front edge at a distance of 0.75m from the wall.
Washbasins must be placed at a height of 0.8m from the floor and equipped with lever faucets.
Mirrors must be tilted, with the lower part not exceeding 1.1m in height and the upper part at a height of 1.8m.
Wall hangers must be at a height of 1.3m from the floor, and warning systems must not exceed 0.5m in height.
Toilet doors must open outwards and have a width of 1m.
Certain toilets differ from Terminal 1 specifications in that they lack access from both sides of the toilet bowl and feature a fixed grab bar.
Airport counters must accommodate passengers with reduced mobility, specifically requiring an appropriate counter height and a free area for frontal approach.
In Terminal 1, only the MyWay service counter meets the required height and frontal approach area specifications; the tourist information and ANA airports information counters do not.
Adapted counters must have a length of at least 0.8m and a height between 0.75m and 0.85m. The frontal approach space must have a minimum height of 0.7m and a minimum depth of 0.3m.
Terminal 2 has one information desk (MyWay service counter), which was closed at the time of evaluation.

section: Ticket and Customer Support Counter Accessibility
text: Ticket sales and customer support counters for TAP (Terminal 1), as well as Portway and Easyjet (Terminal 2), are approximately 1.3m high and lack a frontal approach area.
Airline check-in counters in both Terminal 1 and Terminal 2 fail to meet required dimensions, with a recorded height of 1.2m.
Passengers may request assistance for boarding and deplaning during the check-in process, whether completed online or in person.
Service counters in Terminal 1 public areas, such as restaurants and the post office, do not have accessible dimensions or a frontal approach area for passengers with reduced mobility (PRM).
In Terminal 2 public areas, restaurant and commercial counters lack adequate accessibility adjustments, although the exchange service counter complies with Accessibility law.
Priority assistance counters were not found at Lisbon Airport.
MyWay, ANA, and PT telephones at Lisbon Airport feature keyboards without Braille references or raised characters.
Telephones are equipped with a free area that enables a frontal or lateral approach.
PT telephone coin slots and keyboards are positioned at a height between 1m and 1.3m, as required by law.
ATMs and self-check-in machines use touch screen interfaces and do not provide information in sound version, providing only audible warnings.
The slots for inserting or withdrawing cards and other items on self-service equipment have tapered entries.
As required by law, the insertion and withdrawal area is located between 0.40m and 1.20m in height, and the command and controls area is located between 0.80m and 1.20m.
ATM keyboards are not identified with Braille, although some buttons include tactile references.
Operating buttons on self-check-in machines are not identified with Braille or tactile references.
Neither ATMs nor self-check-in machines provide a frontal approach area.
Post boxes are placed at a height of 1.4m, which exceeds the recommended good practice maximum height of 1.20m.
Aircraft access and the distance between the aircraft and landing/boarding areas are typically covered on foot via stairs, creating locomotion difficulties.
Ambulifts (vehicles with lifting mechanisms) are available to assist in loading and unloading, but they are not always available.
There are no relief areas for service animals at Lisbon airport.
Means of transport adapted for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) allowing access to Oporto airport include taxis, buses, and the metropolitan system.
STCP buses 601, 602, 604, and 3M are classified as accessible for PRM.
Certain taxi companies in the Oporto area, specifically Taxis Pinheiro Lda, operate adapted vehicles.
The Oporto metropolitan and metropolitan airport station are classified as accessible.
Temporary parking spaces for loading and unloading are located near the building door and are identified with the international symbol of access.

Temporary parking spaces meet minimum required dimensions and feature vertical and horizontal signage with the international symbol of access and MyWay service signs, although they are not marked with contrasting colors on the floor surface.
Temporary parking spaces include a shared lateral range access with a width of at least 1m.
An elevator provides access between floors and the reserved parking area of Oporto airport.
In the reserved 51 parking area of Oporto airport, each reserved space has dimensions larger than 5x2.5m.
The number of places reserved for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM), relative to the total number of places for people without reduced mobility, respects the legal requirements.
PRM areas are identified with vertical and horizontal signage featuring the international symbol of access and are marked with contrasting colors on the floor surface.
As recommended in Annex 2, the height of kerbs near pedestrian crossings is not higher than 0.12m.
Near pedestrian crossings, there is a bump of 0.02m between the road surface and the kerb, and these are higher than the road surface.
The minimum width of pavements when obstacles are present is not lower than 1.5m.
Floors must provide good adherence, and the height of fixed signposts is 2m.

The interception of islands in the middle of a zebra crossing with the pedestrian crossings must have a length of 1.5m or more, and the island width must not be less than the width of the pedestrian crossing.
Pedestrian crossing surfaces have a different texture than pavements and roads, but they lack the contrasting color recommended by ACAPO.
There are no traffic lights at the pedestrian crossings.
Circulation areas inside the airport are equipped with stairs, ramps, escalators, and elevators between floors.
There is a lack of tactile signage on the floor, including directional tactile flooring and alert tactile flooring.
There is a lack of Braille materials at information and check-in counters, in commercial areas, and on aircraft safety equipment as per ICAO (2013).
Signage for accessible bathrooms and elevators must feature letters in a contrasting color, be free of brightness, and be positioned at a height of 2m from floor level.
Ramps near temporary parking spaces have a width greater than 0.9m, an inclination of 12%, a gap of 0.12m, and a horizontal projection of 0.8m, meeting Accessibility law requirements.
Stairs must have a width of at least 1.50m, a maximum step height of 0.16m, and a minimum step depth of 0.28m, with dimensions remaining constant across each staircase.

Stairs must be equipped with sideguards and handrails on both sides with a height of 0.85m and a diameter of 0.05m. Handrails must extend 0.3m beyond the last step parallel to the floor and extend beyond the first step by a length equal to the step depth while maintaining the slope.

Stair edges must feature a strip of 0.04m width with a different texture. Current airport stairs lack contrasting colors on these strips and lack a recommended approach area (differing texture or color) at the start of the stairs.
Stairs must contain rest platforms with a depth greater than 1m and a width not less than the width of the steps.
Existing stairs in the airport exhibit sharp edges.
Lifts must meet or exceed a minimum size of 1.10m x 1.40m, with a minimum usable doorway width of 0.90m and an obstacle-free area of at least 1.5m x 1.5m in front of the entrance door.
Call buttons must be at a height of 1.2m. Internal control buttons must be positioned between 0.9m and 1.3m and must include Braille, raised characters, and luminous devices.
Lift cabins must be equipped with grab bars located at a height of 0.9m and 0.05m from the wall.
Existing lifts at Oporto airport lack sound information systems.
At Oporto airport, all doors must have a width of 0.90m or more and must not have revolving handles.
Locks and pullers must be located at a height of 1.05m.
The airport utilizes automatic and revolving doors for outdoor access, although revolving doors are not recommended.
The width of building aisles must be greater than 1.8m.
Waiting room aisles must meet minimum widths of 0.9m and 1.5m. However, there is a noted lack of reserved spaces for passengers in wheelchairs.
Toilets must have a minimum area greater than 2.2m x 2.2m, and doors must open outwards with a width of 1m.
The toilet bowl must be located at a height of 0.45m from the floor, with the front edge at a distance of 0.75m from the wall, allowing access from both sides.
Toilets must feature folding vertical grab bars on both sides at heights of 0.65m and 0.8m, extending approximately 0.20m beyond the front edge of the toilet bowl.
Washbasins must be equipped with sensory taps and placed at a height of 0.8m from the floor.
Mirrors must be fixed, with the lower part exceeding 0.9m in height and the upper part reaching 1.8m.
Wall hangers must be at a height of 1.3m from the floor, and the warning system height must not exceed 0.4m.

Information counters (ANA and MyWay) and ticket sales/customer support counters (TAP, Portway, GroundForce, and EasyJet) must provide appropriate height and area for frontal approach in accordance with the accessibility guide.
Airline check-in counters are identified as non-compliant with required accessibility dimensions, with a current height of 1.2m.
Establishments with service counters, such as post offices and exchange services, feature accessible dimensions and a frontal approach area for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM).
Restaurants, commercial counters, Custom House counters, Viagens Abreu, and Passport Shop lack adequate accessibility adjustments.
MyWay service telephones, ANA airports telephones, and PT telephones lack keyboards with Braille references or raised characters.
Telephones provide a free area that enables either a frontal or lateral approach.
The coin slots and keyboards of PT telephones are positioned at a height between 1m and 1.3m, as required.
ATMs and self-check-in machines utilize touch screen interfaces without sound-version information, providing only audible warnings.
The slots for inserting or withdrawing cards on self-service equipment feature tapered entries.
The insertion and withdrawal area is located between 0.40m and 1.20m in height, and the command and controls area is located between 0.80m and 1.20m, as required.
ATM keyboards are not identified with Braille, although some buttons include tactile references.
Operating buttons on self-check-in machines lack Braille or tactile references.
ATMs and self-check-in machines do not provide a frontal approach area.
Post boxes are positioned at a height of 1.4m, which exceeds the recommended good practice maximum height of 1.20m.
Ambulifts (vehicles with lifting mechanisms for loading and unloading) are not always available.
There are no designated areas available for service animals to relieve themselves at Oporto Airport.
Passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) must arrive at the airport at least two hours before the departure of their flight.
To notify the MyWay service of their arrival, passengers should use the MyWay service telephone located at the meeting point near the entrance of the building.
Passengers with reduced mobility are permitted to bypass the standard check-in queue and move to the head of the line to be immediately attended by check-in staff.
Passengers who have checked in online may notify check-in staff upon arrival to activate the MyWay accessibility service.
The MyWay service coordinates assistant availability via telephone and directs passengers to designated waiting areas until assistance arrives.
Airport wheelchair assistance includes transporting the passenger to security control; accompanying passengers are responsible for managing hand luggage during transit.
