section: Security Screening for Passengers with Disabilities
text: During security hand searches, personnel should inquire about the nature of a passenger's disability before touching affected areas of the body.
Following security clearance, accessibility assistants transport passengers to designated waiting areas in the airside zone and establish a scheduled return time for further assistance.

Passengers may be required to present documentation issued at the check-in counter to receive boarding and deplaning assistance; failure to provide this documentation may result in the refusal of assistance services.
Passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) are entitled to priority boarding to avoid waiting in general passenger lines.
An ambulift is used to facilitate the boarding of passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) who are unable to use stairs.

During boarding via ambulift, the equipment may experience vibrations during ascent and oscillations when the platform is positioned at the aircraft door level, as the platform does not link fully to the aircraft.
Cabin crew may store a passenger's crutches in the cabin baggage area; in such cases, the passenger must notify the crew to retrieve them whenever they need to move from their seat.

Passengers requiring assistance may be required to remain on the aircraft after all other passengers have disembarked while waiting for designated ground assistance (e.g., MyWay assistant) to facilitate deplaning.
Ground assistance staff may suggest or encourage the use of aircraft stairs for disembarkation to increase speed and efficiency, providing physical support to the passenger during the process.

Upon reaching the bottom of the aircraft stairs, passengers are transitioned from the disembarkation assistant to a separate airport wheelchair assistant for transport to the designated departure point.
At Lisbon Airport Terminal 2, the MyWay service counter may be closed, requiring passengers to utilize shuttle services from Terminal 1.

Failures in accessibility include the non-extension of ramps for passengers with reduced mobility (PMR) and the failure of staff to inquire if assistance or ramp deployment is necessary for passengers using mobility aids such as crutches.
Reserved spaces for persons with reduced mobility (PRM) must be kept clear; misuse of these areas by passengers without reduced mobility or for baggage storage creates accessibility barriers.
The Boeing 737-800 aircraft features an aisle width of approximately 0.6m, a space between seats of 0.3m, and an accessible toilet area of 0.55m².
The recommended minimum dimensions for a toilet installed inside a cubicle are 1.6m x 1.7m.

Air transport must adopt a policy of continuous improvement to resolve physical and attitudinal barriers caused by staff and other passengers, as obstacles can occur in all phases of flight and negatively impact the decision of persons with reduced mobility to travel.

section: Impact of Flight Disruptions on PRM
text: For passengers with reduced mobility (PRM), operational constraints such as flight delays, cancellations, or damaged mobility equipment can result in traumatic experiences.

Airport infrastructures should comply with accessibility guides and the ICAO Manual for the accessibility of PRM in air transport, specifically regarding the provision of tactile flooring and Braille information at information counters.

Under Regulation (EC) no. 1107 / 2006, PRM assistance services are required to provide priority boarding and priority access at security checkpoints, as well as ensure the availability of necessary equipment upon landing.

Limitations on mobility and social participation for PRM are driven by failures to comply with accessibility principles in building infrastructures and a lack of adequate training for human resources.
Evaluation of airport infrastructures at Lisbon and Oporto airports was conducted to analyze dimensions and observe physical and architectural barriers.
Identification of attitudinal barriers and constraints in the assistance service for passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) was conducted by accompanying a PRM during a flight.
A structured questionnaire was used to collect perceptions of service needs from people with disabilities to identify constraints within the airport and aircraft.
The survey targeted individuals with disabilities and their companions, collecting 164 responses, including 68 from individuals with reduced mobility and 31 from companions.

Reported disabilities among survey participants were primarily physical impairments (75%) and sensory impairments (15.6%), with the highest incidence of reduced mobility occurring in individuals under the age of 65.
Accessibility policies must take into account the needs of passengers with various types of disabilities, extending beyond locomotor disabilities.
There is a requirement to reinforce legislation to ensure the autonomy of Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) during their journey.
Existing assistance services at airports require greater dissemination, and airports and airlines must increase the availability of accessibility information on their websites.

Key barriers for PRM passengers include the lack of specialized and trained personnel, inadequate airport information and assistance services, and difficulties with transfers within the airport terminal.
Current airport and airline websites are perceived as difficult for PRM passengers to use when seeking information about available services.
Infrastructure at national airports, specifically Lisbon and Oporto, is cited as lacking the necessary conditions to provide an autonomous and comfortable experience for PRM.
Staff training for assisting persons with reduced mobility should be reinforced to include sign language to facilitate communication with passengers with hearing disabilities.
Staff training must be reinforced regarding the handling of hold baggage containing mobility equipment to ensure equipment is not damaged during the journey.

The most significant constraints for passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) on aircraft are boarding/deplaning transfers, space between seats, lavatories, mobility inside the aircraft, and seat transfers.
PRM passengers report a predominantly negative flying experience with Ryanair on national routes compared to other carriers like TAP.
There is a lack of sufficient information regarding evacuation procedures for PRM, resulting in an inability for many to evacuate autonomously during airport emergencies.
There is a requirement for reinforced legislation and the elimination of both airport and aircraft barriers to provide PRM with the same travel comfort as passengers without reduced mobility.
Comprehensive training of staff is identified as an essential requirement to overcome existing accessibility barriers.
Accessible transport options to Lisbon airport include taxis, metropolitan services, and buses.
Passengers using private vehicles for flights at Terminal 2 have no available parking spaces at that terminal and must use parking at Terminal 1.
The P2 car park at Terminal 1 is accessible; however, the temporary parking area lacks the recommended contrasting floor lines.
Passengers who do not go directly to Terminal 2 must utilize the Shuttle T2 for transfer between Terminal 1 and Terminal 2.

The reserved area for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) on shuttles must be maintained and not occupied by passengers without reduced mobility or their luggage, regardless of whether the vehicle is equipped with ramps.
Pavements, kerbs, and pedestrian crossings must comply with the dimensions specified in the accessibility guide.
Pedestrian crossings are recommended by ACAPO to have contrasting colors and traffic lights to ensure secure crossing.

Directional tactile flooring must be installed in the direction of displacement, have a width between 0.20m and 0.60m, and feature a contrasting color compared to the adjacent floor to provide safety, orientation, and mobility to key areas such as information counters.

Alert tactile flooring must be used to signal safety risks, such as uneven surfaces, and to identify the start and end of ramps, stairs, escalators, or lifts. It must have a minimum width of 0.25m, with a recommended width of 0.40m to 0.60m.

According to the ICAO manual (2013), airports must incorporate wayfinding methods for people with disabilities, including visual contrast, detectable patterns on floors and walls indicating direction, and the strategic arrangement of architectural elements like walls and columns.
Information and security signage must be provided in Braille at information counters, check-in counters, shopping areas, and inside aircraft.
Ramps used to overcome height differences between the kerb and the road in Terminal 1 and Terminal 2 are in conformity with Accessibility law.

To correct excessive inclination of ramps between floors in Terminal 1, a rest platform should be installed in the middle, creating two ramps with a 6% gradient, a height of 0.6m, and a horizontal projection of 6m each.

Staircases must include handrails on both sides extending at the base and top, an approach area with a distinct texture and colour, and a 0.04m wide strip with texture and contrasting colour on the edge of the steps.

Lifts must provide sound information and are recommended to have the call button positioned on the right side. Required conformities include specific dimensions, handrails, door width, and call/control button height with tactile references.
All doors, corridors, and waiting rooms are in conformity regarding their dimensions.
According to Annex 2 and the ICAO manual, waiting areas must provide reserved seating for people with special needs, ensuring that seats are not placed where they block evacuation routes.
Toilets in Terminal 1 are accessible to Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM), although one facility has dimensions lower than the recommended standards.
Two irregularities exist in Terminal 2 toilets: there is no access from both sides of the toilet and one of the grab bars is fixed.
Only the MyWay service information counter (Terminal 1) and the exchange counter (Terminal 2) have frontal approach areas with the correct dimensions for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM).
Check-in counters, ticket sales, customer support counters, and restaurant area counters lack frontal approach areas and exceed recommended height requirements.
There is no priority assistance provided under Decreto-Lei 135/99; PRM are required to wait in line as passengers without reduced mobility.
At Lisbon airport, telephones (MyWay service, ANA, or PT) permit frontal or side approach and have slots for coins and keyboards at a suitable height, but the keyboards lack tactile references.
In both ATMs and self-check-in machines, the location of commands, controls, and input/output slots are in conformity.
Self-service machines lack sound-based information transmission and frontal approach areas. Only ATM keyboards are identified with tactile references; self-check-in keyboards are not.
Post boxes meet legal requirements, although good practices recommend a lower height for improved accessibility for PRM.
Vehicles used to assist in boarding and deplaning are not always available, creating barriers for PRM, specifically those with physical or locomotor disabilities.
Relief areas are mentioned in the ICAO manual, although they are not included in the accessibility guide.
Airports must provide dedicated areas available for service animals to relieve themselves.
Airport refurbishment is essential to remove existing barriers, and periodic inspections should be conducted to ensure no new barriers are created.
Accessibility must be ensured for stairs, ramps, lifts, toilets, commercial areas, service counters, parking lots, and airport outside areas.
Airports should provide directional or alert tactile flooring to guide passengers with visual disabilities and replace standard telephones with TTY systems.
Waiting rooms must provide adequate reserved seating for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) that is spacious and comfortable.

Terminal shuttle buses must be accessible, equipped with ramps, and ensure that reserved areas for PRM are respected and not occupied by other passengers. This must be guaranteed through periodic supervision.
Transport means to the airport, including taxis, metropolitan transit, and buses, should be classified as accessible.
Airport parking must provide designated accessible parking spaces for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM).
Temporary parking spaces must have lines with contrasting colors, and ramps used to overcome the difference between the curb and the road must not exceed recommended slope gradients.
Pavements, kerbs, and pedestrian crossings comply with legal requirements regarding dimensions.
Pedestrian crossings lack contrasting colors and traffic lights.
Indoor flooring is appropriate in dimension but lacks tactile flooring for navigation.
While signage exists for toilets, lifts, and other facilities, there is no information provided in Braille.
Stairs feature appropriate dimensions, rest platforms, sideguards, and handrails on both sides that extend to the base and top of the steps.
Stairs lack the recommended floor approach areas characterized by different textures and colors.
Stairs have 0.04m edge strips; however, these strips only provide a different texture and lack the required contrasting color.
Stairs have sharp edges, which pose a danger specifically to passengers with visual disabilities.
Lifts comply with standards for dimensions, handrails, door width, and the height of call and control buttons, including the provision of tactile references.
Lifts lack sound information, and call buttons are occasionally positioned incorrectly.
In airports with multiple floors, lifts must be provided as an alternative to stairs, and ramps must be provided as a secondary alternative to ensure PRM mobility during emergencies or lift failures.
Interior doors, corridors, and waiting rooms meet dimensional conformity standards.
Waiting rooms lack reserved seats for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM), which is a requirement of the accessibility guide and the ICAO manual.
The use of revolving doors for outside entrances is not recommended for accessibility.

Toilets identified with the international symbol of accessibility have necessary adaptations for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) regarding dimensions, taps, toilets, washbasins, grab bars, and alarms. However, the base of the fixed wall mirrors is positioned at a height exceeding 0.9m.
Accessible counters for PRM are available at Information, ticket sales, customer support, currency exchange, and CTT counters.
Check-in counters, restaurant area counters, customs house counters, and passport shop counters are not adapted for PRM.
Existing counters do not provide priority assistance.
Airport telephones (MyWay, ANA, and PT) permit frontal or side approach and have coin slots and keyboards at a suitable height, but the keyboards lack tactile references.
ATMs and self-check-in machines have compliant input/output slots and control locations. Only ATMs feature keyboards with tactile references.
Self-service machines lack audio-based information transmission and a frontal approach area.
Post boxes meet legal requirements, although a lower installation height is recommended to improve PRM accessibility.
Vehicles are available to assist PRM during boarding and deplaning, but these vehicles are not consistently available.
There are no dedicated relief areas for service animals, creating accessibility barriers for visually impaired passengers within the airport circuit and inside aircraft.
To enhance accessibility for people with reduced mobility (PRM), airports should increase the dissemination of information regarding available services, such as the MyWay service.
Accessibility information may be disseminated through promotional videos on airport and airline websites, as well as through information leaflets and materials in Braille at the airport.
Airports must provide information regarding emergency evacuations and adapted transport options to the airport, including accessible bus services and taxi companies with adapted vehicles.

The MyWay system is an assistance service that provides help and equipment for loading and unloading to help passengers overcome airport obstacles. The ELO system consists of infrastructure that allows PRMs to remain autonomous and protected from weather conditions.

Under Regulation (EC) no. 1107/2006, passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) must be able to obtain basic airport information, announce their arrival, and request assistance at the point of assistance service; they should not be required to move to a check-in counter to activate assistance.
The quality commitment of service requires that 80% of passengers should not wait more than 10 minutes for assistance.
According to the ICAO Manual, the service process for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) must ensure there are no points where passengers are left abandoned or unattended.
Assistance must be provided for hand luggage, and security control employees must exercise proper care when inspecting Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM).
In accordance with Regulation (EC) no. 1107/2006, all staff providing assistance to persons with reduced mobility must have the necessary knowledge to meet the needs of PRM.
Restaurant and commercial areas must be adapted and prepared to receive PRMs to eliminate accessibility barriers.

In accordance with Decree Law 135/99 and the ICAO Manual, priority attendance must be given to PRMs. Persons with reduced mobility should be permitted to board the aircraft before other passengers and deplane after them to ensure a more dignified and less stressful experience.
To ensure constant availability, it is recommended that each airline maintains its own loading and unloading equipment for PRMs.
According to ISO15845:2014, boarding vehicles for persons with reduced mobility must offer a sense of comfort to the passenger and must not exhibit equipment oscillations that cause distress.

Currently, seats available for passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) are determined only by the criterion that they are not located near an emergency door; there are no specifically reserved seats for these passengers.
Per ICAO manuals, aircraft must have designated accessible seats for PRM, and these seats should only be occupied by other passengers as a last resort.
Passengers with reduced mobility who are injured (e.g., broken leg) or traveling with a service animal require seats that fulfill specific space requirements to ensure comfortable travel.
Accessible seats for PRM should provide additional space and be reserved near the aircraft entrance/exit doors or lavatories to minimize the distance the passenger must move.
Proposed interior aircraft refurbishments include the creation of more spacious toilets, wider aisles, and increased space between seats.

section: Visual Disability and Service Animal Provisions
text: Aircraft refurbishments should include directional tactile signage in corridors to guide passengers with visual disabilities and a designated relief area for service animals.
The unavailability of ambulifts during deplaning at Lisbon and Oporto airports, forcing passengers to use stairs, is contrary to the intended purpose of boarding and deplaning assistance services.

Adequate assistance must be provided at the airport and inside the aircraft to support the specific needs of passengers with reduced mobility, utilizing necessary personnel and all required equipment.
Barriers in flight phases include the lack of information provided in Braille and the unavailability of personnel specialized in sign language.
According to the ICAO manual, guidance material regarding air transport must be published using appropriate formats and technologies accessible to various types of disabilities.

Specialized staff are required to address the specific needs of passengers based on their type of reduced mobility, including specialized personnel for the handling of baggage and mobility equipment for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM).
Negative attitudes toward disability from staff and other passengers are identified as major barriers during all phases of flight.
The absence of designated relief areas during all phases of flight causes significant inconvenience for service animals.

During online ticket reservation, passengers must be made aware of assistance services so they can select the necessary options and choose appropriate seating. Seats selected for passengers with reduced mobility cannot be adjacent to an emergency door.
For reservations made at the airport, counter employees must inform and assist passengers in requesting special assistance and selecting appropriate seating.
Airport counters are not always adapted to meet the needs of passengers with reduced mobility.
Passengers who do not request assistance are required to navigate airport barriers independently, which may involve traveling significant distances without physical preparation.
Passengers must have access to accessible means of transport and accessible parking spaces when arriving at the airport.
Airport exterior areas, including pavements, kerbs, pedestrian crossings, and entry/exit doors, must be accessible and free of barriers.
There is a limited number of seats available for passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) per flight; aircraft may reach maximum capacity for passengers requiring assistance.
Airport buses transporting passengers between terminals must be accessible to passengers with reduced mobility (PRM).
Airports must provide adequate signage and directional/alert tactile flooring to guide passengers with visual disabilities to adapted information counters or telephone assistance services.
Adequate signage is required to enable passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) to locate Myway points for service activation.
Telephone assistance services must be equipped with TTY (Teletypewriter) and tactile references to accommodate passengers with hearing and visual impairments.
Passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) face a lack of information regarding emergency procedures for autonomous evacuation.
PRMs are unable to move between floors in airports where lifts or ramps are not provided as alternatives to stairs.
PRMs must notify the airport 48 hours in advance of the flight and must arrive at the airport at least 2 hours before departure.
During the check-in phase, an accompanying person or a medical certificate may be required.
Boarding can be refused if the passenger is not prepared for the necessary check-in requirements.
Check-in counters may lack necessary height adaptations and priority attendance spaces, exposing PRMs to indiscreet looks from other passengers.
According to the ICAO manual, service counters should be accessible to all persons with reduced mobility.
Airport and aircraft operators must ensure that check-in kiosks are accessible and identified with the universal symbol of accessibility.
Wait times for service assistants during passport control and security inspections, combined with a lack of information, constitute a major barrier for PRMs.
Waiting rooms must provide reserved seating for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) to ensure they can rest comfortably while awaiting staff assistance.
Airport wheelchairs must be available and maintained in good condition. Assistance services should include support with the handling of hold baggage.
Security inspections for PRM require priority attendance and staff who are specifically qualified to perform inspections for passengers with reduced mobility.
The carriage of hand luggage throughout the airport is a required function of the assistant service to ensure PRMs or their companions are not burdened with carrying luggage.
Airside and landside facilities, including bathrooms, commercial areas, and ATMs, must be adjusted for accessibility, specifically requiring frontal approach access for ATMs.
Boarding priority must be strictly respected for PRM, and necessary equipment must be available to facilitate the boarding process.
The transfer from an airport wheelchair to an aisle chair requires that the aisle chair be available, in good condition, and suitable for the passenger.
Onboard aircraft, barriers include narrow corridors, small bathrooms, and reduced space between seats, which impact the comfort of passengers with reduced mobility.
Aircraft should provide reserved seating for passengers with special needs, ensuring they are not placed far from entrance/exit doors or bathrooms.
Passengers with Reduced Mobility (PRM) cannot be seated adjacent to the emergency door as they may be unable to evacuate autonomously in the event of an emergency.
PRMs are responsible for bringing their hand luggage into the passenger cabin unless accompanied by another person or assisted by a MyWay service assistant.

To overcome communication barriers for passengers with visual or hearing limitations, information should be provided in Braille and sign language, and tactile directional signage should be placed throughout the cabin per ICAO manuals.
Barriers for PRMs during the post-flight stage include waiting times for MyWay service assistance and a lack of available equipment to assist with landing/boarding.
In the absence of available assistance equipment, passengers are required to descend the aeroplane stairs on foot.
MyWay service assistants provide necessary support to help PRMs navigate barriers at passport control, hold baggage reclaim, and customs.
PRMs may encounter accessibility failures in arrival area facilities, including bathrooms, waiting rooms, commercial areas, ATMs, telephones, lifts, stairs, and ramps.
Constraints for PRMs include a lack of adequate signage, a lack of directional or alert tactile flooring, and a deficiency in emergency evacuation procedures.
To reduce barriers in outside airport areas, it is necessary to implement recommended standards for pavements, kerbs, pedestrian crossings, and entry/exit doors.

Passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) require accessible means of transportation from the airport, including the provision of accessible parking spaces, to facilitate their departure from the airport.

Key constraints identified at airports include a lack of specialized and trained personnel, deficiencies in airport information and assistance services, and barriers during transfers within the terminal and at baggage claim.
A lack of clear information regarding evacuation procedures prevents passengers with reduced mobility from being able to evacuate the airport autonomously in the event of an emergency.
Main accessibility constraints within the aircraft include the boarding and deplaning transfer process, insufficient space between seats, inadequate lavatories, and limited mobility inside the cabin.

Specific issues at Lisbon Airport include inefficient transfers between Terminal 1 and Terminal 2 via the shuttle bus, excessive waiting times and poor quality of assistance services, and a lack of adapted service counters.
The toilet facilities in Lisbon Airport Terminal 2 require improvements to ensure accessibility.
Passengers with reduced mobility should be granted priority assistance, specifically the opportunity to board the aircraft before other passengers and deplane after them.
At Oporto airport, check-in counters are not in conformity with accessibility standards due to having a height greater than recommended.
Revolving doors and the lack of available boarding and unloading equipment constitute significant barriers for passengers with reduced mobility.
Oporto and Lisbon airports provide accessible parking lots and means of transport to the airport classified as accessible.
Irregularities have been identified in the implementation of ramps and stairs.
Waiting rooms currently lack reserved seating for passengers with reduced mobility (PRM); it is required to provide designated seating in waiting areas for people with special needs.
Airports must provide tactile flooring and tactile references on telephones and self-service equipment for passengers with visual disabilities.
Accessibility barriers have been identified in the pre-travel, pre-flight, and post-flight phases of travel.
The rights of passengers with reduced mobility within the European Union are ensured by Regulation (EC) no. 1107/2006, which applies to all Member States.
If the rights of passengers with reduced mobility are not respected, passengers should contact the corresponding airline or airport, or submit a formal complaint.
In Portugal, the Accessibility law establishes normative provisions to remove barriers in infrastructures, including airports, to improve the quality of life for people with reduced mobility.
A person with reduced mobility (PRM) is defined as a person whose mobility is reduced due to a physical or psychological limitation, whether temporary or permanent.

Failures in PRM assistance services include the disrespect for priority at security checkpoints, the failure of priority boarding as referred to in Regulation (EC) no. 1107/2006, and the lack of equipment available during deplaning.

Accessibility issues identified at Lisbon airport include problems with transfers between airports, excessive waiting times for assistance services, poor quality of assistance, a lack of adapted service counters, and the disrespect for priority boarding.
Accessibility issues identified at Oporto airport include improper height of check-in counters, the use of revolving doors, and a lack of available equipment for boarding and unloading.

To eliminate existing accessibility barriers, there is a requirement for the reinforcement of legislation and the implementation of periodic audits to verify and ensure that new barriers are not created.

To promote air travel participation, it is necessary to identify and address physical and attitudinal barriers, as well as intrinsic decision-making variables, that inhibit travel or limit satisfaction for people with reduced mobility (PRM).
Continuous accessibility training for all staff working with people with reduced mobility (PRM) is critical to ensuring improved assistance and service delivery.
Airport operators must implement measures and reconstruction plans for infrastructure to resolve existing accessibility problems.
The removal of architectural barriers must involve people with disabilities in the project design phase to ensure that interventions are correctly implemented.
Legislation protecting the rights of citizens with disabilities or reduced mobility should be developed from the viewpoint of these individuals and must be fully applied.
Airports and airlines must increase the availability of information regarding services for people with reduced mobility (PRM) on their official websites.

Future research aims to extend accessibility studies to national and international airports to compare barriers, design airports completely free of physical barriers for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM), identify issues within assistance services for PRM, and develop new solutions to resolve existing airport accessibility problems.
Regulation (CE) no 261/2004 establishes common rules on compensation and assistance to passengers in the event of denied boarding and of cancellation or long delay of flights.

section: EU Rights for Persons with Reduced Mobility
text: Regulation (EC) no 1107/2006 defines the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility when travelling by air.
The ICAO Manual on Access to Air Transport by Persons with Disabilities (Doc 9984, First Edition 2013) provides the framework for accessibility in air transport.
Research identifies specific accessibility constraints within airport terminal facilities that impact passengers with reduced mobility.
Constraints exist within airport emergency protocols specifically regarding the evacuation and safety of passengers with reduced mobility.
Infraero Aeroportos utilizes the Accessibility-ELO system for managing airport accessibility.
ISO 15845:2014 establishes the functional and safety requirements for aircraft ground equipment, specifically boarding vehicles for persons with reduced mobility.
The SNRIPD (2009) guide provides interpretative notes for Decree-Law (DL) 163/2006 regarding accessibility and mobility for all.
MyWay (2008) provides guidance and assistance procedures for persons with reduced mobility.
Airport Cooperative Research Program Synthesis 64 addresses the issues and requirements related to accommodating animals travelling through airports.
Airport Cooperative Research Program Synthesis 51 analyzes the impacts and requirements associated with aging travelers in airport environments.
The survey aims to explore and identify the main constraints that compromise the rights of Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) and factors that reduce their willingness to use air transportation.
All responses provided in the survey regarding accessibility constraints in air transportation are anonymous and confidential.

The survey collects data on the physical and psychological limitations of passengers with reduced mobility and assesses whether previous negative experiences have led passengers to avoid specific airports or airlines.
Assessment of whether Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) are capable of autonomously evacuating airport facilities during emergency situations.
Requirement for available and accessible information regarding airport emergency plans, evacuation procedures, and emergency exits specifically for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM).

Identification of operational constraints faced by Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) within airport environments, specifically regarding: Airport Information and Assistance Services, Baggage Claim, Boarding Areas, Check-in, Duty-Free Shops, Emergency Exits, Lavatories, Security, Terminal Transfers, and Waiting Areas.
Identification of constraints related to the lack of specialized and trained personnel to assist Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) within the airport.

Main constraints faced by Passengers with Reduced Mobility (PRM) within the aircraft include accommodating carry-on baggage, boarding and deplaning, transfers, lack of specialized and trained personnel, lavatory access, mobility inside the aircraft, seat belt usage, seat transfers, and the space between seats.
Evaluation of the ease of obtaining information about services for Passengers with Reduced Mobility (PRM) through airport and airline websites.
Assessment of the need for regulatory changes requiring airports and airlines to be obligated to guarantee a specific level of autonomy for Passengers with Reduced Mobility (PRM).
Evaluation of whether air transport is viewed as a transportation mode of easy access for Passengers with Reduced Mobility (PRM).
The maximum incline of pavements and surrounding access ways to buildings is 6% in the longitudinal direction and 2% in the transversal direction.
The height of kerbs near pedestrian crossings must be 0.12m, and 0.02m across the entire width of the pedestrian crossing.
The minimum width of pavements should be 1.5m.
Pavements must have an anti-slip surface.
The minimum height of signs fixed on posts, walls, or other structures is 2m.
Islands in the middle of zebra crossings should have a length of 1.2m or more (1.5m recommended), and the island width must not be less than the width of the pedestrian crossing.
Pedestrian crossing surfaces must have a contrasting color and a different texture compared to the materials used on pavements and roads.
The colour recommended by ACAPO is bordeaux.
Traffic lights must be installed at pedestrian crossings.
Green lights at pedestrian crossings must remain open long enough to allow safe passage at a speed of 2m/5s.
Traffic lights must include additional audible signals, and the activation button must be positioned at a height between 0.8m and 1.2m.
Ramps should be designed with the minimum possible incline.

Ramp specifications based on slope: 6% slope allows a maximum height difference of 0.60m and a maximum horizontal projection of 10.00m; 8% slope allows a maximum height difference of 0.40m and a maximum horizontal projection of 5.00m; 10% slope allows a maximum height difference of 0.20m and a maximum horizontal projection of 2.00m; 12% slope allows a maximum height difference of 0.10m and a maximum horizontal projection of 0.83m.

Horizontal rest platforms (minimum 1.5m length and width not less than the ramp width) are required when the horizontal projection exceeds the specified limit for the slope, and at any change of direction with an angle equal to or less than 90 degrees.
Ramps must have a minimum width of 1.2m, or 0.9m if the ramp has a horizontal projection of less than 5m and two ramps are provided for the same route.

Ramps must feature double handrails on both sides: one positioned between 0.9m and 0.95m and another between 0.7m and 0.75m. Handrails must extend 0.3m at the base and top, remain parallel to the ramp, and have rounded ends.

Handrails are not required if the gradient is 0.2m or less. If the gap is between 0.2m and 0.4m and the slope does not exceed 6%, handrails are only required on one side at a height between 0.85m and 0.95m.
Ramp floors must be constructed from material with good grip and must include lateral protection between 0.05m and 0.1m in height along the entire length.
There should be an area with different texture and contrasting colour in the beginning and the end of the ramps.
For curved ramps, the minimum radius of curvature is 3m, and the slope must be equal to or less than 8%.
Stairs must have a width of at least 1.50m (or 1.2m if providing access to the interior of the building) and must feature a surface with good grip.
Stairs must have sideguards and handrails on both sides with a height of 0.85m or 0.90m and a diameter of 0.04m or 0.05m.
Handrails should extend 0.3m beyond the last step, parallel to the floor.
At the base of the stairs, handrails should extend beyond the first step by a length equal to the step depth, maintaining the slope of the stairs.
At the start of the stairs, there must be a yellow approach area with a different texture, measuring 0.6m in width and extending 0.5m away from the first step.
The edge of steps must feature a slip-resistant strip with a different texture and colour contrast, with a width of at least 0.04m.
Stairs must have a maximum height of 0.18m between steps and a minimum depth of 0.28m; these dimensions must remain constant throughout the staircase.
Stairs exceeding 2.4m in vertical rise must include rest platforms with a depth greater than 0.7m and a width at least equal to the width of the steps.
All components of the stairs shall be free of sharp edges or any points of possible danger.
All doors should have a width of 0.90m or more and must not have revolving handles.
Locks and pullers should be located at a height between 0.9m and 1.10m.
The use of revolving doors should be avoided.
The lift car must have a minimum area of 1.10m x 1.40m, and the doorway must have a minimum usable width of 0.8m.
In front of the entrance door there should be an obstacle-free area equal to or greater than 1.5m x 1.5m.
The call buttons should be located on the right side of the door, at a height of 1.2m.
The control buttons inside the lift should be between 0.9m and 1.3m of height.
Buttons should have tactile references in Braille, raised characters, and luminous devices around them.
Inside the cabin, there should be grab bars located at a height of 0.9m and 0.05m from the wall.
It is recommended as good practice that lifts have sound information in addition to visual information.
The service counter should have an adapted area with a length of no less than 0.80m and a height between 0.75m and 0.85m.
A frontal approach area must be provided with a height of not less than 0.7m and a depth of not less than 0.3m.
If the interface is a touch screen, the information should also be transmitted in a sound version.
Slots for inserting or withdrawing coins/cards must have tapered entries.
The insertion and withdrawal products area must be located between 0.40m and 1.20m of height, and the command and controls area must be at a height between 0.80m and 1.20m.
The keyboard should be identified with tactile references, in Braille or raised characters.
For frontal approach to self-service equipment, there must be a free space providing a frontal approach area with not less than 0.7m of height and a depth of not less than 0.3m.
Post boxes should be placed at a height between 0.6m and 1.4m, although a maximum height of 1.20m is recommended as good practice.
Telephones must have keyboards with tactile references, in Braille or raised characters.
There must be a free area for a frontal or lateral approach. Suspended telephones must have a free area with a width of 0.7m or more and a height of 0.65m or more.
The slot for coins/cards and the keyboard must be positioned at a height between 1m and 1.3m.
Toilets must have a minimum area of 2.2m x 2.2m. For cabin toilets, the minimum area required is 1.6m x 1.7m.
Access must be provided from both sides of the toilet bowl, with foldable vertical supports installed on both sides at a height of 0.7m.
Grab bars must extend beyond the front edge of the toilet bowl by a distance between 0.20m and 0.45m.
The toilet bowl must be 0.45m above the floor, and the front edge must be 0.75m from the wall to facilitate wheelchair transfers.
The washbasin must be installed at a height between 0.7m and 0.8m.
Taps must be either lever-operated or sensor-activated faucets.
For tilted mirrors, the base maximum height is 1.1m. For vertically fixed mirrors, the base maximum height is 0.9m. The top of mirrors must be at a minimum height of 1.8m.
Wall hangers must be installed at a height of 1.3m from the floor.
Warning systems must be positioned between 0.4m and 0.6m in height to ensure accessibility for wheelchair users or persons who have fallen.
Toilet doors must either open outwards or be sliding doors.
Building aisles must have a dimension of 1.8m or greater.
Aisle widths in waiting rooms must meet the minimum specifications of 0.9m and 1.5m.
Waiting rooms must include reserved spaces for passengers in wheelchairs with a minimum area of 1m x 1.5m.

The number of seats reserved for Persons with Reduced Mobility (PRM) is determined by total capacity: 3 seats for up to 300 seats; 5 seats for 301-1000 seats; and 5 seats for every 1000 seats for capacities above 1000.
When parking spaces are located above or below street-level, access must be guaranteed via ramp and lift.
Reserved parking spaces for PRM must have minimum dimensions of 5m x 2.5m.

Reserved PRM spaces must be marked on the floor with a contrasting color. Identification must include a horizontal accessibility sign of at least 1m painted in a contrasting color on the floor and a vertical sign featuring the international symbol of accessibility.
Reserved parking spaces must provide lateral range access with a minimum width of 1m; this access width may be shared by two adjacent spaces.

The number of reserved parking spaces for PRM is based on the total number of spaces: 1 space for up to 10 spaces; 2 spaces for 11-25 spaces; 3 spaces for 26-100 spaces; 4 spaces for 101-500 spaces; and 1 space for every 100 spaces for totals above 500.

Due to global population growth and the aging process, the percentage of people with reduced mobility is expected to increase significantly, necessitating increased investment in air transport accessibility.
Existing improvements in accessibility services and airport facilities should be expanded to encompass all airports.
The objective is to explore the service needs of passengers with disabilities and identify the factors that inhibit them from using air transport.
Major obstacles for passengers with reduced mobility are located at the land-air interface, specifically within terminals, aircraft boarding and deplaning points, airport entrances, and taxi areas.
